1.ESV:
1. James, a servant of God and of the Lord Jesus Christ, To the twelve tribes in the Dispersion: Greetings.
2. Count it all joy, my brothers, when you meet trials of various kinds,
3. for you know that the testing of your faith produces steadfastness.
4. And let steadfastness have its full effect, that you may be perfect and complete, lacking in nothing.
5. If any of you lacks wisdom, let him ask God, who gives generously to all without reproach, and it will be given him.
6. But let him ask in faith, with no doubting, for the one who doubts is like a wave of the sea that is driven and tossed by the wind.
7. For that person must not suppose that he will receive anything from the Lord;
8. he is a double-minded man, unstable in all his ways.
9. Let the lowly brother boast in his exaltation,
10. and the rich in his humiliation, because like a flower of the grass he will pass away.
11. For the sun rises with its scorching heat and withers the grass; its flower falls, and its beauty perishes. So also will the rich man fade away in the midst of his pursuits.
12. Blessed is the man who remains steadfast under trial, for when he has stood the test he will receive the crown of life, which God has promised to those who love him.
13. Let no one say when he is tempted, “I am being tempted by God,” for God cannot be tempted with evil, and he himself tempts no one.
14. But each person is tempted when he is lured and enticed by his own desire.
15. Then desire when it has conceived gives birth to sin, and sin when it is fully grown brings forth death.
16. Do not be deceived, my beloved brothers.
17. Every good gift and every perfect gift is from above, coming down from the Father of lights, with whom there is no variation or shadow due to change.
18. Of his own will he brought us forth by the word of truth, that we should be a kind of firstfruits of his creatures.
19. Know this, my beloved brothers: let every person be quick to hear, slow to speak, slow to anger;
20. for the anger of man does not produce the righteousness of God.
21. Therefore put away all filthiness and rampant wickedness and receive with meekness the implanted word, which is able to save your souls.
22. But be doers of the word, and not hearers only, deceiving yourselves.
23. For if anyone is a hearer of the word and not a doer, he is like a man who looks intently at his natural face in a mirror.
24. For he looks at himself and goes away and at once forgets what he was like.
25. But the one who looks into the perfect law, the law of liberty, and perseveres, being no hearer who forgets but a doer who acts, he will be blessed in his doing.
26. If anyone thinks he is religious and does not bridle his tongue but deceives his heart, this person’s religion is worthless.
27. Religion that is pure and undefiled before God the Father is this: to visit orphans and widows in their affliction, and to keep oneself unstained from the world.

1.notes:
James - Chapter 1
Bible Study Notes
========================================


CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
James tells believers to count trials as joy because testing produces endurance, warns that God tempts no one, declares that every good gift comes from above, and commands: "be doers of the word, and not hearers only, deceiving your own selves."


AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: James (v.1) — most likely James the brother of Jesus (Matt 13:55,
Gal 1:19), who became the leader of the Jerusalem church (Acts 15:13, 21:18).
He was not one of the twelve apostles but became a believer after the
resurrection (1 Cor 15:7). Known as "James the Just" for his piety. Martyred
in AD 62 according to Josephus.

Recipient: "The twelve tribes which are scattered abroad" (v.1) — Jewish
Christians dispersed throughout the Roman Empire. Written approximately AD
45-49, making James possibly the earliest NT book.

Purpose: Practical wisdom for daily Christian living. James is the "Proverbs
of the NT" — short, punchy, practical. He addresses trials, wisdom, the
tongue, favoritism, faith and works, prayer, and more. His concern: faith
that works, not just faith that talks.

Theme: Genuine faith produces visible fruit. "Be ye doers of the word, and
not hearers only" (v.22). Faith without works is dead (2:26).


COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
 https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/james-1/
 Guzik covers trials and joy, asking for wisdom, the source of temptation,
 hearing and doing the Word, and pure religion.

Charles Spurgeon:
 Sermon: "Count It All Joy" on vv.2-4 (MTP Vol. 11, No. 643)
 "COUNT it all joy — not feel it, count it. It's a decision, not an emotion.
 When trials come — not if but when — decide that this is joy. Why? Because
 the trying of your faith worketh patience. Trials are the gymnasium of
 faith. You don't build muscle without resistance. You don't build endurance
 without trials. Count it joy because of what it produces."
 Sermon: "Doers of the Word" on v.22 (MTP Vol. 28, No. 1672)
 "Doers, not hearers only. The man who hears and doesn't do is self-deceived.
 He thinks he's spiritual because he knows truth — but knowledge without
 action is delusion. The mirror illustration is perfect: you look, you see
 the problem, you walk away and forget. That's hearing without doing. The
 doer looks, sees, and ACTS. He is blessed in his deed."


TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.2               | KJV         | "temptations" — Greek "peirasmois" — trials, tests. Same word can mean temptation (to sin) or trial (testing of faith). Context here = trials/tests. KJV: "Divers" / ESV: "various kinds" — various kinds.
v.2               | ESV         | "trials of various kinds" — Greek "peirasmois" — trials, tests. Same word can mean temptation (to sin) or trial (testing of faith). Context here = trials/tests. KJV: "Divers" / ESV: "various kinds" — various kinds.
v.3               | KJV         | "trying of your faith worketh patience" — Greek "dokimion... hupomonēn" — the proving/testing produces endurance/steadfastness. ESV "steadfastness" captures the active endurance better than KJV "patience."
v.3               | ESV         | "testing of your faith produces steadfastness" — Greek "dokimion... hupomonēn" — the proving/testing produces endurance/steadfastness. ESV "steadfastness" captures the active endurance better than KJV "patience."
v.4               | KJV         | "perfect and entire, wanting nothing" — Greek "teleioi kai holoklēroi" — mature and complete, lacking nothing. Trials produce completeness.
v.4               | ESV         | "perfect and complete, lacking in nothing" — Greek "teleioi kai holoklēroi" — mature and complete, lacking nothing. Trials produce completeness.
v.5               | KJV         | "liberally" — Greek "haplōs" — generously, simply, without reservation. KJV: "Upbraideth not" / ESV: "without reproach" — doesn't scold, doesn't make you feel bad for asking.
v.5               | ESV         | "generously" — Greek "haplōs" — generously, simply, without reservation. KJV: "Upbraideth not" / ESV: "without reproach" — doesn't scold, doesn't make you feel bad for asking.
v.8               | KJV         | "double minded" — Greek "dipsuchos" — two-souled. A word James may have coined. Divided loyalty, wavering between faith and doubt.
v.8               | ESV         | "double-minded" — Greek "dipsuchos" — two-souled. A word James may have coined. Divided loyalty, wavering between faith and doubt.
v.14              | KJV         | "drawn away of his own lust, and enticed" — Fishing/hunting imagery. "Drawn away" (exelkomenos) = dragged out. "Enticed" (deleazomenos) = baited, lured. Sin uses our own desires as bait.
v.14              | ESV         | "lured and enticed by his own desire" — Fishing/hunting imagery. "Drawn away" (exelkomenos) = dragged out. "Enticed" (deleazomenos) = baited, lured. Sin uses our own desires as bait.
v.17              | KJV         | "Father of lights" — God as creator of celestial lights (sun, moon, stars). KJV: "No variableness, neither shadow of turning" / ESV: "no variation or shadow due to change" — unlike celestial bodies that shift and cast shadows, God never changes.
v.17              | ESV         | "Both equivalent." — God as creator of celestial lights (sun, moon, stars). KJV: "No variableness, neither shadow of turning" / ESV: "no variation or shadow due to change" — unlike celestial bodies that shift and cast shadows, God never changes.
v.21              | KJV         | "engrafted word" — Greek "emphutos logos" — implanted word. The Word planted within like a seed that grows and saves. KJV: "superfluity of naughtiness" / ESV: "rampant wickedness" — Greek "perisseia kakias" — overflow of evil/wickedness.
v.21              | ESV         | "implanted word" — Greek "emphutos logos" — implanted word. The Word planted within like a seed that grows and saves. KJV: "superfluity of naughtiness" / ESV: "rampant wickedness" — Greek "perisseia kakias" — overflow of evil/wickedness.
v.25              | KJV         | "perfect law of liberty" — The gospel/new covenant as a law that frees rather than binds. Perfect because it accomplishes what the old law could not.
v.25              | ESV         | "perfect law, the law of liberty" — The gospel/new covenant as a law that frees rather than binds. Perfect because it accomplishes what the old law could not.
v.27              | KJV         | "fatherless" — Greek "orphanous." KJV: "unspotted from the world" / ESV: "unstained from the world" — Both equivalent in meaning. Pure religion defined by compassion and holiness.
v.27              | ESV         | "orphans" — Greek "orphanous." KJV: "unspotted from the world" / ESV: "unstained from the world" — Both equivalent in meaning. Pure religion defined by compassion and holiness.
v.2               | NET         | "Consider it all joy, my brothers and sisters, when you encounter various trials" — "various" (poikilois = many-colored/diverse). WEB: "Count it all joy, my brothers, when you fall into various temptations." KJV: "divers temptations." Trials come in many varieties — each produces different aspects of maturity.
v.5               | NET         | "God, who gives to all generously and without reprimand" — "without reprimand" (mE oneidizontos = without reproaching/shaming). WEB: "who gives to all liberally and without reproach." KJV: "upbraideth not." God doesn't shame us for asking — He gives without making us feel guilty.
v.14              | NET         | "each one is tempted when he is lured and enticed by his own desires" — "lured and enticed" (exelkomenos kai deleazomenos = dragged away and baited — fishing metaphor). WEB: "drawn away by his own lust, and enticed." Our desires are both the bait and the hook.
v.17              | NET         | "Every good and perfect gift is from above, coming down from the Father of lights" — "Father of lights" (patros tOn phOtOn = Father of luminaries/heavenly bodies). WEB: "the Father of lights." KJV: "the Father of lights." God created the stars and is Himself pure light — no shadow.
v.22              | NET         | "But be sure you live out the message and do not merely listen to it" — "live out" (poiEtai logou = doers of the word). WEB: "be doers of the word." KJV: "be ye doers of the word." The central command of James — faith produces action or it is not real faith.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                                                                  | Notes
------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Count it all joy  | A deliberate decision to view trials as occasions for                                                              | 
Trying of faith   | The testing/proving of faith through trials. Like refining                                                         | 
Double minded     | Divided in loyalty, wavering between faith and doubt.                                                              | 
Drawn away and enticed| The process of temptation: internal desire (lust)                                                                  | 
Father of lights  | God as the unchanging source of all good. Unlike shifting                                                          | 
Engrafted/Implanted word| Scripture planted within the believer like a seed.                                                                 | 
Doers of the word | Those who act on what they hear. Not just hearers                                                                  | 
Pure religion     | True faith expressed in caring for the vulnerable and                                                              | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "Count it all joy when ye fall into divers temptations" (v.2). What
   trial are you currently facing? Can you "count" it joy — not feel it but
   decide it — because of what God is producing through it?

2. "If any of you lack wisdom, let him ask" (v.5). Do you need wisdom for a
   decision, a relationship, a situation? Ask God — He gives generously and
   without scolding. Have you asked?

3. "Swift to hear, slow to speak, slow to wrath" (v.19). How are you doing
   with these three? Do you listen more than you talk? Do you control your
   anger? Which one needs the most work?

4. "Be ye doers of the word, and not hearers only" (v.22). What truth have
   you heard recently that you haven't acted on? What would it look like to
   DO it this week?

5. "Pure religion... to visit the fatherless and widows" (v.27). Is your
   faith producing care for the vulnerable? Who in your life needs
   practical help that you could provide?

2.ESV:
1. My brothers, show no partiality as you hold the faith in our Lord Jesus Christ, the Lord of glory.
2. For if a man wearing a gold ring and fine clothing comes into your assembly, and a poor man in shabby clothing also comes in,
3. and if you pay attention to the one who wears the fine clothing and say, “You sit here in a good place,” while you say to the poor man, “You stand over there,” or, “Sit down at my feet,”
4. have you not then made distinctions among yourselves and become judges with evil thoughts?
5. Listen, my beloved brothers, has not God chosen those who are poor in the world to be rich in faith and heirs of the kingdom, which he has promised to those who love him?
6. But you have dishonored the poor man. Are not the rich the ones who oppress you, and the ones who drag you into court?
7. Are they not the ones who blaspheme the honorable name by which you were called?
8. If you really fulfill the royal law according to the Scripture, “You shall love your neighbor as yourself,” you are doing well.
9. But if you show partiality, you are committing sin and are convicted by the law as transgressors.
10. For whoever keeps the whole law but fails in one point has become guilty of all of it.
11. For he who said, “Do not commit adultery,” also said, “Do not murder.” If you do not commit adultery but do murder, you have become a transgressor of the law.
12. So speak and so act as those who are to be judged under the law of liberty.
13. For judgment is without mercy to one who has shown no mercy. Mercy triumphs over judgment.
14. What good is it, my brothers, if someone says he has faith but does not have works? Can that faith save him?
15. If a brother or sister is poorly clothed and lacking in daily food,
16. and one of you says to them, “Go in peace, be warmed and filled,” without giving them the things needed for the body, what good is that?
17. So also faith by itself, if it does not have works, is dead.
18. But someone will say, “You have faith and I have works.” Show me your faith apart from your works, and I will show you my faith by my works.
19. You believe that God is one; you do well. Even the demons believe—and shudder!
20. Do you want to be shown, you foolish person, that faith apart from works is useless?
21. Was not Abraham our father justified by works when he offered up his son Isaac on the altar?
22. You see that faith was active along with his works, and faith was completed by his works;
23. and the Scripture was fulfilled that says, “Abraham believed God, and it was counted to him as righteousness”—and he was called a friend of God.
24. You see that a person is justified by works and not by faith alone.
25. And in the same way was not also Rahab the prostitute justified by works when she received the messengers and sent them out by another way?
26. For as the body apart from the spirit is dead, so also faith apart from works is dead.
2.KJV:
1. My brethren, have not the faith of our Lord Jesus Christ, the Lord of glory, with respect of persons.
2. For if there come unto your assembly a man with a gold ring, in goodly apparel, and there come in also a poor man in vile raiment;
3. And ye have respect to him that weareth the gay clothing, and say unto him, Sit thou here in a good place; and say to the poor, Stand thou there, or sit here under my footstool:
4. Are ye not then partial in yourselves, and are become judges of evil thoughts?
5. Hearken, my beloved brethren, Hath not God chosen the poor of this world rich in faith, and heirs of the kingdom which he hath promised to them that love him?
6. But ye have despised the poor. Do not rich men oppress you, and draw you before the judgment seats?
7. Do not they blaspheme that worthy name by the which ye are called?

2.notes:
James - Chapter 2
Bible Study Notes
========================================


CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
James condemns showing favoritism to the rich over the poor, declares that faith without works is dead — "as the body without the spirit is dead, so faith without works is dead also" — using Abraham and Rahab as examples of active faith.


AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: James, the brother of Jesus (see Chapter 1 notes for full details).

Historical Context: Chapter 2 addresses two related issues: favoritism in the
church (vv.1-13) and the relationship between faith and works (vv.14-26). The
early church included both rich and poor, and the temptation to favor the
wealthy was real. James condemns this as incompatible with faith in Christ.
He then tackles the most debated question in James: Can faith without works
save? His answer: No. Genuine faith always produces works. Faith and works are
not opposed — they are inseparable.

Favoritism Condemned (vv.1-13): Don't show partiality based on wealth (vv.1-4).
God chose the poor to be rich in faith (v.5). The rich often oppress believers
(vv.6-7). The "royal law" — love your neighbor — condemns favoritism (v.8).
Partiality is sin (v.9). Breaking one point of the Law makes you a lawbreaker
(vv.10-11). Live as those judged by "the law of liberty" (v.12). "Mercy
rejoiceth against judgment" (v.13).

Faith and Works (vv.14-26): Can faith without works save? (v.14). Faith that
doesn't help the needy is useless (vv.15-16). "Faith, if it hath not works,
is dead" (v.17). Show me your faith BY your works (v.18). Even demons believe
— and tremble (v.19). Abraham was "justified by works" when he offered Isaac
(vv.21-23). Rahab was justified by works (v.25). "Faith without works is dead"
(v.26).

Note: James does not contradict Paul. Paul speaks of justification BEFORE GOD
(declared righteous by faith alone, Rom 3:28). James speaks of justification
BEFORE MEN (demonstrated righteous by works, v.18). Paul addresses how we are
saved; James addresses how we know we are saved.


COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
 https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/james-2/
 Guzik covers favoritism, the royal law, the faith/works relationship, and
 the harmony between James and Paul.

Charles Spurgeon:
 Sermon: "Faith and Works" on vv.17-18 (MTP Vol. 18, No. 1078)
 "Faith without works is dead — not weak, not sick, not sleeping — DEAD.
 A dead faith is no faith at all. It is a corpse of faith — it has the form
 but not the life. How do you know faith is alive? By its works. As a tree
 is known by its fruit, faith is known by its works. James does not say
 works save you — he says works PROVE you are saved. Show me your faith BY
 your works. Faith that doesn't work doesn't exist."
 Sermon: "Mercy Rejoiceth Against Judgment" on v.13 (MTP Vol. 11, No. 624)
 "Mercy rejoiceth — it triumphs, it exults, it overcomes. Against judgment —
 when mercy and judgment meet, mercy wins. Not that judgment is unjust but
 that mercy is greater. The merciful man will receive mercy. The unmerciful
 man will receive strict judgment. Show mercy — and mercy will triumph over
 the judgment you deserve."


TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1               | KJV         | "respect of persons" — Greek "prosōpolēmpsia" — face-receiving, judging by external appearance. Favoritism based on outward status.
v.2               | KJV         | "assembly" — Greek "sunagōgēn" — synagogue. James uses the Jewish term for their gathering place, reflecting the early Jewish- Christian context.
v.2               | ESV         | "Both equivalent." — Greek "sunagōgēn" — synagogue. James uses the Jewish term for their gathering place, reflecting the early Jewish- Christian context.
v.8               | KJV         | "royal law" — Greek "nomon basilikon" — the king's law, the supreme law. Love of neighbor is the law that rules over all other laws.
v.8               | ESV         | "Both equivalent." — Greek "nomon basilikon" — the king's law, the supreme law. Love of neighbor is the law that rules over all other laws.
v.14              | KJV         | "can faith save him?" — Greek "mē dunatai hē pistis sōsai auton?" — The Greek expects a negative answer: "That kind of faith can't save him, can it?" The article "hē" (THE faith) points to the specific faith just described — faith without works.
v.14              | ESV         | "faith save" — Greek "mē dunatai hē pistis sōsai auton?" — The Greek expects a negative answer: "That kind of faith can't save him, can it?" The article "hē" (THE faith) points to the specific faith just described — faith without works.
v.19              | KJV         | "the devils also believe" — Demons have correct theology — they believe God is one. But their belief produces trembling, not obedience. Intellectual assent without life change is not saving faith.
v.21              | KJV         | "justified by works" — Greek "edikaiōthē ex ergōn" — declared righteous from/by works. James uses "justified" in the sense of "demonstrated to be righteous" (before men), not "declared righteous" (before God, as Paul uses it).
v.21              | ESV         | "Both equivalent." — Greek "edikaiōthē ex ergōn" — declared righteous from/by works. James uses "justified" in the sense of "demonstrated to be righteous" (before men), not "declared righteous" (before God, as Paul uses it).
v.22              | KJV         | "faith wrought with his works" — Greek "sunērgei" — worked together with, cooperated. Faith and works are partners, not enemies. "Made perfect" — Greek "eteleiōthē" — brought to completion. Works complete/perfect faith by expressing it.
v.22              | ESV         | "works, and faith" — Greek "sunērgei" — worked together with, cooperated. Faith and works are partners, not enemies. "Made perfect" — Greek "eteleiōthē" — brought to completion. Works complete/perfect faith by expressing it.
v.26              | KJV         | "the body without the spirit is dead" — The analogy: as a body without breath/spirit is a corpse, faith without works is a corpse. Works are to faith what breath is to the body — the evidence of life.
v.26              | ESV         | "body apart from the spirit is dead" — The analogy: as a body without breath/spirit is a corpse, faith without works is a corpse. Works are to faith what breath is to the body — the evidence of life.
v.1               | NET         | "do not show prejudice" — "prejudice" (prosOpolEmpsiais = face-receiving/favoritism). WEB: "don't show partiality." KJV: "have not the faith... with respect of persons." Faith in Jesus and favoritism are incompatible — you cannot hold both simultaneously.
v.14              | NET         | "What good is it, my brothers and sisters, if someone claims to have faith but does not have works?" — "claims" (legE = says). WEB: "if a man says he has faith." KJV: "if a man say he hath faith." The emphasis: SAYS vs. SHOWS. Verbal faith without visible works is suspect.
v.19              | NET         | "You believe that God is one; well and good. Even the demons believe that — and tremble with fear" — "tremble" (phrissousin = shudder/have hair stand on end). WEB: "the demons also believe, and shudder." Even demons have correct theology — belief alone is not saving faith.
v.23              | NET         | "Abraham believed God and it was credited to him as righteousness" — quoting Genesis 15:6 (same text Paul uses in Romans 4). WEB: "it was accounted to him as righteousness." James and Paul are not contradicting but complementing — Paul addresses the ground of justification; James addresses its evidence.
v.26              | NET         | "as the body without the spirit is dead, so also faith without works is dead" — "without the spirit" (chOris pneumatos = apart from breath/spirit). WEB: "faith apart from works is dead." An analogy: works are to faith what breath is to the body — the evidence of life, not its cause.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                                                                  | Notes
------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Respect of persons| Favoritism, partiality. Judging people by external                                                                 | 
Royal law         | "Love your neighbor as yourself." The supreme law that                                                             | 
Mercy rejoiceth against judgment| Mercy triumphs over strict justice. The                                                                            | 
Faith without works is dead| Faith that produces no visible change is not                                                                       | 
Justified by works| Demonstrated to be righteous through visible actions.                                                              | 
Devils believe    | Intellectual assent to truth without life transformation.                                                          | 
Faith wrought with works| Faith and works cooperating as partners. Works                                                                     | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "Have not the faith... with respect of persons" (v.1). Do you treat
   people differently based on their wealth, appearance, or status? How
   would Jesus treat the poorly dressed person who walks into your church?

2. "Mercy rejoiceth against judgment" (v.13). Are you merciful toward
   others? Or do you demand strict justice for everyone except yourself?
   The measure of mercy you give is the measure you'll receive.

3. "Faith, if it hath not works, is dead" (v.17). Examine your faith: what
   visible evidence does it produce? If someone looked at your life, would
   they see living faith or dead religion?

4. "The devils also believe, and tremble" (v.19). Is your faith merely
   intellectual agreement with facts about God? Or does it produce
   obedience, love, and transformation? What's the difference between your
   faith and a demon's?

5. "Shew me thy faith by thy works" (v.18). If someone asked you to prove
   your faith, what would you point to? What works demonstrate that your
   faith is alive?

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — James Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/james/

David Guzik — James 2 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/james-2/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

========================================
Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles Spurgeon (Public Domain)

3.ESV:
1. Not many of you should become teachers, my brothers, for you know that we who teach will be judged with greater strictness.
2. For we all stumble in many ways. And if anyone does not stumble in what he says, he is a perfect man, able also to bridle his whole body.
3. If we put bits into the mouths of horses so that they obey us, we guide their whole bodies as well.
4. Look at the ships also: though they are so large and are driven by strong winds, they are guided by a very small rudder wherever the will of the pilot directs.
5. So also the tongue is a small member, yet it boasts of great things. How great a forest is set ablaze by such a small fire!
6. And the tongue is a fire, a world of unrighteousness. The tongue is set among our members, staining the whole body, setting on fire the entire course of life, and set on fire by hell.
7. For every kind of beast and bird, of reptile and sea creature, can be tamed and has been tamed by mankind,
8. but no human being can tame the tongue. It is a restless evil, full of deadly poison.
9. With it we bless our Lord and Father, and with it we curse people who are made in the likeness of God.
10. From the same mouth come blessing and cursing. My brothers, these things ought not to be so.
11. Does a spring pour forth from the same opening both fresh and salt water?
12. Can a fig tree, my brothers, bear olives, or a grapevine produce figs? Neither can a salt pond yield fresh water.
13. Who is wise and understanding among you? By his good conduct let him show his works in the meekness of wisdom.
14. But if you have bitter jealousy and selfish ambition in your hearts, do not boast and be false to the truth.
15. This is not the wisdom that comes down from above, but is earthly, unspiritual, demonic.
16. For where jealousy and selfish ambition exist, there will be disorder and every vile practice.
17. But the wisdom from above is first pure, then peaceable, gentle, open to reason, full of mercy and good fruits, impartial and sincere.
18. And a harvest of righteousness is sown in peace by those who make peace.

7. For every kind of animal, bird, creeping thing, and sea creature, is tamed, and has been tamed by mankind;
8. but nobody can tame the tongue. It is a restless evil, full of deadly poison.
9. With it we bless our God and Father, and with it we curse men, who are made in the image of God.
10. Out of the same mouth comes blessing and cursing. My brothers, these things ought not to be so.
11. Does a spring send out from the same opening fresh and bitter water?
12. Can a fig tree, my brothers, yield olives, or a vine figs? Thus no spring yields both salt water and fresh water.
13. Who is wise and understanding among you? Let him show by his good conduct that his deeds are done in gentleness of wisdom.
14. But if you have bitter jealousy and selfish ambition in your heart, don’t boast and don’t lie against the truth.
15. This wisdom is not that which comes down from above, but is earthly, sensual, and demonic.
16. For where jealousy and selfish ambition are, there is confusion and every evil deed.
17. But the wisdom that is from above is first pure, then peaceful, gentle, reasonable, full of mercy and good fruits, without partiality, and without hypocrisy.
18. Now the fruit of righteousness is sown in peace by those who make peace.
3.notes:
James - Chapter 3
Bible Study Notes
========================================


CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
James warns about the untamable power of the tongue — a small member that sets the whole course of life on fire — and contrasts earthly wisdom (bitter jealousy and selfish ambition) with heavenly wisdom that is pure, peaceable, and full of mercy.


AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: James, the brother of Jesus (see Chapter 1 notes for full details).

Historical Context: Chapter 3 addresses two topics: the power and danger of
the tongue (vv.1-12) and two kinds of wisdom (vv.13-18). The tongue section
is one of the most vivid and convicting passages in Scripture — using six
metaphors to describe the tongue's disproportionate power. The wisdom section
contrasts earthly wisdom (characterized by envy and strife) with heavenly
wisdom (characterized by purity and peace).

The Tongue (vv.1-12): Don't rush to be teachers — greater accountability
(v.1). We all stumble in speech (v.2). Six metaphors: (1) a bit controlling
a horse (v.3), (2) a rudder steering a ship (v.4), (3) a small fire starting
a forest blaze (vv.5-6), (4) an untameable wild animal (vv.7-8), (5) a spring
producing both fresh and salt water (vv.11), (6) a tree bearing wrong fruit
(v.12). The tongue is "a fire, a world of iniquity" (v.6), "set on fire of
hell" (v.6), "full of deadly poison" (v.8).

Two Kinds of Wisdom (vv.13-18): Earthly wisdom: bitter envy, selfish ambition,
boasting, lying (vv.14-16). It is "earthly, sensual, devilish" (v.15) and
produces "confusion and every evil work" (v.16). Heavenly wisdom: pure,
peaceable, gentle, reasonable, full of mercy, impartial, sincere (v.17). It
produces "the fruit of righteousness... sown in peace" (v.18).


COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
 https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/james-3/
 Guzik covers the tongue's power, the six metaphors, the inconsistency of
 blessing and cursing, and the two wisdoms.

Charles Spurgeon:
 Sermon: "The Tongue" on vv.5-8 (MTP Vol. 30, No. 1800)
 "A little member — but what destruction it causes! A spark — but what a
 forest it burns! No man can tame it — that is the terrifying truth. You
 cannot tame your own tongue by willpower. Only the Holy Spirit can bridle
 what no human effort can control. The tongue reveals the heart — and the
 heart needs transformation, not just the tongue."
 Sermon: "Heavenly Wisdom" on v.17 (MTP Vol. 25, No. 1470)
 "First pure — purity comes first. Then peaceable — peace built on purity,
 not compromise. Gentle — not harsh or demanding. Easy to be intreated —
 approachable, reasonable, willing to yield. Full of mercy — not stingy with
 compassion. Good fruits — productive, not barren. Without partiality —
 consistent, not playing favorites. Without hypocrisy — genuine, not fake.
 This is wisdom from above — and it looks nothing like the world's wisdom."


TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1               | KJV         | "masters" — Greek "didaskaloi" — teachers. Don't rush to become teachers — they face stricter judgment. Teaching carries weight.
v.1               | ESV         | "teachers" — Greek "didaskaloi" — teachers. Don't rush to become teachers — they face stricter judgment. Teaching carries weight.
v.2               | KJV         | "offend" — Greek "ptaiō" — to stumble, to trip. "Perfect" — Greek "teleios" — mature, complete. Tongue control indicates overall maturity.
v.2               | ESV         | "stumble" — Greek "ptaiō" — to stumble, to trip. "Perfect" — Greek "teleios" — mature, complete. Tongue control indicates overall maturity.
v.6               | KJV         | "course of nature" — Greek "trochos tēs geneseōs" — the wheel of existence, the cycle of life. The tongue affects the entire course of human life. "Set on fire of hell" — Greek "geennēs" — Gehenna. The tongue's destructive power has a hellish source.
v.6               | ESV         | "entire course of life" — Greek "trochos tēs geneseōs" — the wheel of existence, the cycle of life. The tongue affects the entire course of human life. "Set on fire of hell" — Greek "geennēs" — Gehenna. The tongue's destructive power has a hellish source.
v.8               | KJV         | "no man can tame" — Human effort alone cannot control the tongue. Only divine power (the Spirit) can accomplish what human willpower cannot. KJV: "unruly evil" / ESV: "restless evil" — Greek "akatastaton kakon" — unstable, restless evil.
v.8               | ESV         | "no human being can tame" — Human effort alone cannot control the tongue. Only divine power (the Spirit) can accomplish what human willpower cannot. KJV: "unruly evil" / ESV: "restless evil" — Greek "akatastaton kakon" — unstable, restless evil.
v.15              | KJV         | "earthly, sensual, devilish" — Three descending levels: earthly (epigeos — from below, not above), sensual/unspiritual (psuchikē — natural, not of the Spirit), devilish/demonic (daimoniōdēs). Worldly wisdom has a demonic origin.
v.15              | ESV         | "earthly, unspiritual, demonic" — Three descending levels: earthly (epigeos — from below, not above), sensual/unspiritual (psuchikē — natural, not of the Spirit), devilish/demonic (daimoniōdēs). Worldly wisdom has a demonic origin.
v.17              | KJV         | "easy to be intreated" — Greek "eupeithēs" — compliant, willing to yield. KJV: "without partiality" / ESV: "impartial" — Greek "adiakritos." KJV: "without hypocrisy" / ESV: "sincere" — Greek "anupokritos." Eight characteristics of heavenly wisdom listed in both translations with equivalent meaning.
v.17              | ESV         | "open to reason" — Greek "eupeithēs" — compliant, willing to yield. KJV: "without partiality" / ESV: "impartial" — Greek "adiakritos." KJV: "without hypocrisy" / ESV: "sincere" — Greek "anupokritos." Eight characteristics of heavenly wisdom listed in both translations with equivalent meaning.
v.18              | KJV         | "the fruit of righteousness is sown in peace of them that make peace" — Both equivalent in meaning. Greek "karpos dikaiosunēs" — fruit/harvest of righteousness.
v.18              | ESV         | "a harvest of righteousness is sown in peace by those who make peace" — Both equivalent in meaning. Greek "karpos dikaiosunēs" — fruit/harvest of righteousness.
v.2               | NET         | "For we all stumble in many ways" — "stumble" (ptaiomen = trip/fail/make mistakes). WEB: "For in many things we all stumble." KJV: "For in many things we offend all." James includes himself — tongue control is universal struggle. No one has mastered it.
v.6               | NET         | "the tongue is a fire, the world of unrighteousness among our members" — "world of unrighteousness" (ho kosmos tEs adikias). WEB: "a world of iniquity." KJV: "a world of iniquity." The tongue contains an entire cosmos of evil — small in size, infinite in destructive capacity.
v.8               | NET         | "But no human being can subdue the tongue" — "subdue" (damasai = tame — used of wild animals). WEB: "no man can tame the tongue." KJV: "the tongue can no man tame." We can tame lions and dolphins — but not the tongue. Only God can.
v.17              | NET         | "the wisdom from above is first pure, then peaceable, gentle, accommodating, full of mercy and good fruit" — "accommodating" (eupeithEs = easily persuaded/open to reason). WEB: "gentle, reasonable." KJV: "easy to be intreated." Heavenly wisdom is not stubborn or combative.
v.18              | NET         | "the fruit of righteousness is sown in peace among those who make peace" — "sown in peace" (en eirEnE speiretai). WEB: "the fruit of righteousness is sown in peace by those who make peace." Righteousness grows in peaceful conditions — conflict is infertile soil.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                                                                  | Notes
------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
The tongue as fire| The tongue's ability to cause massive destruction from                                                             | 
No man can tame   | The tongue is beyond human control. Only the Spirit can                                                            | 
Set on fire of hell| The tongue's destructive power has a demonic source.                                                               | 
Earthly, sensual, devilish| Three levels of worldly wisdom's origin: from                                                                      | 
Wisdom from above | Divine wisdom characterized by purity, peace, gentleness,                                                          | 
Fruit of righteousness| The harvest produced by peacemakers who sow in                                                                     | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "If any man offend not in word, the same is a perfect man" (v.2). How is
   your speech? Do you gossip, criticize, complain, lie, or wound with
   words? Tongue control is the measure of maturity.

2. "The tongue can no man tame" (v.8). Have you tried to control your
   tongue by willpower alone? It doesn't work. Are you depending on the
   Spirit to do what you cannot?

3. "Out of the same mouth proceedeth blessing and cursing" (v.10). Do you
   praise God on Sunday and curse people on Monday? What inconsistencies
   exist in your speech?

4. Review the characteristics of heavenly wisdom (v.17). Which ones
   describe you? Which ones don't? Are you operating from earthly wisdom
   (envy, ambition, strife) or heavenly wisdom (purity, peace, mercy)?

5. "The fruit of righteousness is sown in peace" (v.18). Are you a
   peacemaker? Righteousness grows in peaceful soil. Where do you need to
   sow peace instead of strife?

------------------------------------

4.notes:
James - Chapter 4
Bible Study Notes
========================================


CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
James exposes the source of quarrels (unfulfilled desires), calls worldliness spiritual adultery, commands "submit yourselves to God, resist the devil and he will flee from you," and warns against presumptuous planning that ignores God's sovereignty.


AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: James, the brother of Jesus (see Chapter 1 notes for full details).

Historical Context: Chapter 4 diagnoses the root cause of conflict (internal
desires, vv.1-3), warns against worldliness (vv.4-6), calls for repentance
and humility (vv.7-10), condemns judgmental speech (vv.11-12), and rebukes
presumptuous planning (vv.13-17). The chapter is blunt and confrontational —
James pulls no punches about the spiritual condition of his readers.

The Source of Conflict (vv.1-3): Wars and fights come from internal lusts
(v.1). You want but don't have; you don't have because you don't ask; you ask
but don't receive because you ask selfishly (vv.2-3).

Worldliness Condemned (vv.4-6): Friendship with the world = enmity with God
(v.4). You cannot be both God's friend and the world's friend. But God gives
"more grace" (v.6) — He resists the proud but gives grace to the humble.

The Call to Repentance (vv.7-10): Ten imperatives in rapid succession: submit
to God, resist the devil, draw near to God, cleanse your hands, purify your
hearts, be afflicted, mourn, weep, turn laughter to mourning, humble
yourselves (vv.7-10). Result: "He shall lift you up" (v.10).

Against Judging (vv.11-12): Don't speak evil of brothers — you're not the
judge; God is (vv.11-12).

Against Presumption (vv.13-17): Don't boast about tomorrow — life is a vapor
(v.14). Say "If the Lord will" (v.15). Knowing good and not doing it is sin
(v.17).


COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
 https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/james-4/
 Guzik covers the source of conflict, worldliness, the ten imperatives,
 judging, and presumptuous planning.

Charles Spurgeon:
 Sermon: "Draw Nigh to God" on v.8 (MTP Vol. 17, No. 1025)
 "Draw nigh to God — the initiative is yours. AND HE WILL draw nigh to you
 — the response is His. God does not play hard to get. He responds to every
 genuine approach. But you must move first. Not because He is unwilling but
 because He honors your freedom. Take one step toward God and He takes ten
 toward you. Draw nigh — in prayer, in worship, in obedience, in surrender."
 Sermon: "Life a Vapour" on v.14 (MTP Vol. 14, No. 815)
 "A vapour — a mist, a breath on a cold morning. It appears for a moment
 and vanishes. That is your life. Not a mountain — a vapour. Not an ocean —
 a breath. You are here today and gone tomorrow. This is not morbid — it is
 realistic. And it should produce urgency: if life is short, live it well.
 If life is a vapour, don't waste it on vanity."


TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1               | KJV         | "lusts that war in your members" — Greek "hēdonōn" — pleasures, desires for pleasure. Root of English "hedonism." Internal desires wage war and produce external conflicts.
v.1               | ESV         | "your passions are at war within you" — Greek "hēdonōn" — pleasures, desires for pleasure. Root of English "hedonism." Internal desires wage war and produce external conflicts.
v.4               | KJV         | "adulterers and adulteresses" — Spiritual adultery — unfaithfulness to God by loving the world. OT imagery of Israel as God's unfaithful wife (Hosea, Ezekiel 16). ESV uses gender-neutral "people" for the collective address.
v.4               | ESV         | "adulterous people" — Spiritual adultery — unfaithfulness to God by loving the world. OT imagery of Israel as God's unfaithful wife (Hosea, Ezekiel 16). ESV uses gender-neutral "people" for the collective address.
v.5               | KJV         | "the spirit that dwelleth in us lusteth to envy" — Difficult verse with varied translations. Likely: God's Spirit yearns jealously for our undivided devotion.
v.5               | ESV         | "He yearns jealously over the spirit that he has made to dwell in us" — Difficult verse with varied translations. Likely: God's Spirit yearns jealously for our undivided devotion.
v.6               | KJV         | "more grace" — Greek "meizona charin" — greater grace. However strong our sin, God's grace is stronger. But it flows to the humble, not the proud.
v.6               | ESV         | "Both equivalent." — Greek "meizona charin" — greater grace. However strong our sin, God's grace is stronger. But it flows to the humble, not the proud.
v.7               | KJV         | "resist the devil, and he will flee" — The promise: active resistance (in God's strength, after submission to God) causes Satan to flee. He cannot stand against a submitted, resisting believer.
v.7               | ESV         | "Both equivalent." — The promise: active resistance (in God's strength, after submission to God) causes Satan to flee. He cannot stand against a submitted, resisting believer.
v.8               | KJV         | "double minded" — Greek "dipsuchoi" — two-souled (same word as 1:8). Divided between God and the world. The cure: purify your hearts.
v.8               | ESV         | "double-minded" — Greek "dipsuchoi" — two-souled (same word as 1:8). Divided between God and the world. The cure: purify your hearts.
v.9               | KJV         | "Be afflicted, and mourn, and weep" — Greek "talaipōrēsate" — be miserable, feel the weight of your sin. A call to genuine repentance, not superficial sorrow.
v.9               | ESV         | "Be wretched and mourn and weep" — Greek "talaipōrēsate" — be miserable, feel the weight of your sin. A call to genuine repentance, not superficial sorrow.
v.14              | KJV         | "vapour" — Greek "atmis" — steam, mist, vapor. Visible briefly, then gone. The most vivid biblical image of life's brevity.
v.14              | ESV         | "mist" — Greek "atmis" — steam, mist, vapor. Visible briefly, then gone. The most vivid biblical image of life's brevity.
v.17              | KJV         | "knoweth to do good, and doeth it not" — Both equivalent in meaning. Sins of omission. Not just doing wrong but failing to do right. Knowledge creates responsibility.
v.17              | ESV         | "whoever knows the right thing to do and fails to do it" — Both equivalent in meaning. Sins of omission. Not just doing wrong but failing to do right. Knowledge creates responsibility.
v.2               | NET         | "You desire and do not have; you murder and covet and are not able to obtain" — "murder" (phoneuete) — some manuscripts read "envy" (phthoneite). WEB: "You kill, and covet." KJV: "Ye kill, and desire to have." Whether literal murder or metaphorical is debated — James uses extreme language.
v.4               | NET         | "Adulterers, do you not know that friendship with the world is hostility toward God?" — "Adulterers" (moichalides = adulteresses, feminine). WEB: "You adulteresses!" KJV: "Ye adulterers and adulteresses." The OT image of spiritual infidelity — Israel as God's unfaithful wife.
v.6               | NET         | "God opposes the proud, but he gives grace to the humble" — quoting Proverbs 3:34 (LXX). "Opposes" (antitassetai = sets himself in battle array against). WEB: "God resists the proud." God actively resists the proud — military language.
v.7               | NET         | "resist the devil and he will flee from you" — "resist" (antistEte = stand against/oppose). WEB: "Resist the devil, and he will flee from you." KJV: "Resist the devil, and he will flee from you." A promise: resistance produces retreat. The devil cannot endure determined opposition.
v.14              | NET         | "you do not know about tomorrow. What is your life like?" — "What is your life?" (poia gar hE zOE hymOn). "You are a puff of smoke" (atmis = vapor/mist). WEB: "you are a vapor." KJV: "It is even a vapour." Life's brevity as morning mist — visible briefly, then gone.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                                                                  | Notes
------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Friendship with the world| Adopting the world's values, priorities, and                                                                       | 
God resisteth the proud| God actively opposes the arrogant. Pride repels                                                                    | 
Submit to God     | Voluntary surrender to God's authority. The prerequisite                                                           | 
Resist the devil  | Active opposition to Satan's temptations and schemes.                                                              | 
Draw nigh to God  | Intentional approach to God through prayer, worship, and                                                           | 
Life is a vapour  | The brevity and uncertainty of human life. Here briefly,                                                           | 
Sins of omission  | Knowing the right thing to do and failing to do it.                                                                | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "From whence come wars and fightings among you?" (v.1). What conflicts
   are you currently in? Can you trace them to internal desires — wanting
   something you don't have? What's the real root?

2. "The friendship of the world is enmity with God" (v.4). Where are you
   trying to be friends with both God and the world? You can't have both.
   What worldly values have you adopted that conflict with God's?

3. "Submit yourselves therefore to God. Resist the devil" (v.7). Are you
   submitted to God? That's the prerequisite for effective resistance.
   Where do you need to submit before you can resist?

4. "Draw nigh to God, and he will draw nigh to you" (v.8). Are you drawing
   near or drifting away? What practical step can you take today to draw
   closer to God?

5. "What is your life? It is even a vapour" (v.14). Life is short and
   uncertain. Are you living with urgency? Are you presuming on tomorrow?
   What would you do differently if you truly believed life is a vapor?

6. "To him that knoweth to do good, and doeth it not, to him it is sin"
   (v.17). What good do you know you should be doing but aren't? Inaction
   is sin when you know the right thing.

5.notes:
James - Chapter 5
Bible Study Notes
========================================


CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
James pronounces woe on the oppressive rich, calls for patient endurance until the Lord's coming, forbids swearing oaths, and instructs the sick to call the elders for prayer and anointing — "the effectual fervent prayer of a righteous man availeth much."


AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: James, the brother of Jesus (see Chapter 1 notes for full details).

Historical Context: Chapter 5 concludes James with warnings to the rich
(vv.1-6), a call to patience until Christ's return (vv.7-11), instructions
about oaths (v.12), and the power of prayer (vv.13-18). The letter ends with
a call to restore wandering believers (vv.19-20). The chapter balances stern
warning (to oppressive rich) with tender encouragement (to suffering believers).

Warning to the Rich (vv.1-6): Weep and howl — judgment is coming (v.1). Your
wealth is rotting (vv.2-3). You defrauded workers — their cries reached God
(v.4). You lived in luxury while condemning the righteous (vv.5-6). This is
not against all wealthy people but against those who gained wealth through
oppression and lived selfishly.

Patience Until the Lord's Coming (vv.7-11): Be patient like a farmer waiting
for harvest (v.7). The Lord's coming is near (v.8). Don't grumble against
each other (v.9). Follow the prophets' example of patience (v.10). Remember
Job's endurance and God's compassionate outcome (v.11).

Oaths (v.12): Let your yes be yes and your no be no. Simple honesty without
elaborate oaths.

The Power of Prayer (vv.13-18): Afflicted? Pray. Cheerful? Sing. Sick? Call
the elders to pray and anoint with oil (vv.13-14). "The prayer of faith shall
save the sick" (v.15). Confess sins to one another and pray for each other
(v.16). "The effectual fervent prayer of a righteous man availeth much" (v.16).
Elijah's example: his prayer stopped and started rain (vv.17-18).

Restoring the Wandering (vv.19-20): If someone wanders from truth and is
brought back, the restorer "shall save a soul from death, and shall hide a
multitude of sins" (v.20).


COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
 https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/james-5/
 Guzik covers the warning to the rich, patience, prayer for the sick,
 confession, and restoring the wandering.

Charles Spurgeon:
 Sermon: "The Effectual Fervent Prayer" on v.16 (MTP Vol. 12, No. 707)
 "The effectual fervent prayer — not cold, formal, routine prayer. Effectual
 — it works, it accomplishes something. Fervent — hot, passionate, energized.
 Of a RIGHTEOUS man — not a perfect man but a right-with-God man. Availeth
 MUCH — not a little, much. Prayer is not a formality — it is a force. It
 moves the hand that moves the world. Elijah was a man like us — with the
 same weaknesses, the same passions. But he prayed, and heaven responded."
 Sermon: "Patience" on vv.7-8 (MTP Vol. 14, No. 826)
 "Be patient — as the farmer waits for rain and harvest. He cannot hurry
 the seasons. He plants, he waits, he trusts. So with us: we plant
 faithfulness, we wait for the Lord's coming, we trust His timing. The
 coming of the Lord draweth nigh — it is closer today than yesterday. Every
 day brings it nearer. Stablish your hearts — settle them, firm them up.
 He is coming."


TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.4               | KJV         | "Lord of sabaoth" — Greek "kuriou sabaōth" — Lord of hosts/armies. A title emphasizing God's power to execute justice. The workers' cries reach the Commander of heaven's armies.
v.4               | ESV         | "Lord of hosts" — Greek "kuriou sabaōth" — Lord of hosts/armies. A title emphasizing God's power to execute justice. The workers' cries reach the Commander of heaven's armies.
v.7               | KJV         | "early and latter rain" — The Palestinian agricultural cycle: early rain (October-November) for planting, latter rain (March-April) for ripening. Both necessary for harvest.
v.7               | ESV         | "early and the late rains" — The Palestinian agricultural cycle: early rain (October-November) for planting, latter rain (March-April) for ripening. Both necessary for harvest.
v.11              | KJV         | "patience of Job" — Greek "hupomonēn Iōb" — the endurance of Job. Not passive patience but active endurance through suffering. KJV: "the Lord is very pitiful, and of tender mercy" / ESV: "the Lord is compassionate and merciful" — Greek "polusplagchnos... oiktirmōn" — very compassionate and merciful.
v.11              | ESV         | "steadfastness of Job" — Greek "hupomonēn Iōb" — the endurance of Job. Not passive patience but active endurance through suffering. KJV: "the Lord is very pitiful, and of tender mercy" / ESV: "the Lord is compassionate and merciful" — Greek "polusplagchnos... oiktirmōn" — very compassionate and merciful.
v.14              | KJV         | "anointing him with oil" — Greek "aleipsantes elaiō" — rubbing with oil. Could be: (1) medicinal (oil as medicine), (2) symbolic (representing the Spirit's work), or (3) sacramental. Combined with prayer — both physical and spiritual care.
v.14              | ESV         | "Both equivalent." — Greek "aleipsantes elaiō" — rubbing with oil. Could be: (1) medicinal (oil as medicine), (2) symbolic (representing the Spirit's work), or (3) sacramental. Combined with prayer — both physical and spiritual care.
v.15              | KJV         | "the prayer of faith shall save the sick" — Both equivalent. "Save" (sōsei) can mean physical healing or spiritual salvation. "The Lord shall raise him up" — God does the healing; prayer is the means.
v.15              | ESV         | "the prayer of faith will save the one who is sick" — Both equivalent. "Save" (sōsei) can mean physical healing or spiritual salvation. "The Lord shall raise him up" — God does the healing; prayer is the means.
v.16              | KJV         | "effectual fervent prayer" — Greek "deēsis energoumenē" — a prayer that is energized, working, active. Not passive words but Spirit-empowered petition. KJV: "availeth much" / ESV: "has great power" — Greek "ischyei polu" — is very strong.
v.16              | ESV         | "the prayer of a righteous person has great power as it is working" — Greek "deēsis energoumenē" — a prayer that is energized, working, active. Not passive words but Spirit-empowered petition. KJV: "availeth much" / ESV: "has great power" — Greek "ischyei polu" — is very strong.
v.17              | KJV         | "subject to like passions" — Greek "homoiopathēs" — of similar nature/feelings. Elijah was human like us — not a superhero. His power was in prayer, not in himself.
v.17              | ESV         | "a man with a nature like ours" — Greek "homoiopathēs" — of similar nature/feelings. Elijah was human like us — not a superhero. His power was in prayer, not in himself.
v.20              | KJV         | "hide a multitude of sins" — Both equivalent. Restoring a wanderer covers/prevents the sins that would have accumulated had they continued wandering. Cf. 1 Peter 4:8.
v.20              | ESV         | "cover a multitude of sins" — Both equivalent. Restoring a wanderer covers/prevents the sins that would have accumulated had they continued wandering. Cf. 1 Peter 4:8.
v.7               | NET         | "Be patient, then, brothers and sisters, until the Lord's return" — "patient" (makrothymEsate = be long- tempered/endure without retaliation). WEB: "Be patient, therefore, brothers." KJV: "Be patient therefore, brethren." The farmer waits for rain — patience is not passive but purposeful waiting.
v.12              | NET         | "do not swear, either by heaven or by earth or by any other oath. But let your 'Yes' be yes and your 'No' be no" — "let your yes be yes" (hEtO hymOn to nai nai). WEB: "let your 'yes' be 'yes'." Simple truthfulness removes the need for oaths — your word should be enough.
v.14              | NET         | "Is anyone among you ill? He should call the elders of the church... and anoint him with oil in the name of the Lord" — "anoint" (aleipsantes = rubbing with oil). WEB: "anointing him with oil." KJV: "anointing him with oil." Whether medicinal (olive oil as medicine) or sacramental is debated — likely both.
v.16              | NET         | "The prayer of a righteous person has great power in its effects" — "great power in its effects" (poly ischyei energoumenE = is strong when it works/is energized). WEB: "The insistent prayer of a righteous person is powerfully effective." KJV: "the effectual fervent prayer of a righteous man availeth much." Prayer that works is both fervent AND righteous.
v.20              | NET         | "whoever turns a sinner back from his wandering path will save that person's soul from death and will cover a multitude of sins" — "cover a multitude of sins" (kalypsei plEthos hamartiOn). WEB: "will cover a multitude of sins." Whose sins? The sinner's? The rescuer's? Likely the converted sinner's — restoration through compassion.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                                                                  | Notes
------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Lord of Sabaoth   | Lord of hosts/armies. God as the Commander of heaven's                                                             | 
Early and latter rain| The two essential rains in Palestine's agricultural                                                                | 
Patience of Job   | Active endurance through suffering, trusting God's                                                                 | 
Prayer of faith   | Prayer offered with genuine trust in God's ability and                                                             | 
Effectual fervent prayer| Spirit-energized, passionate prayer that                                                                           | 
Confess your faults| Mutual confession among believers for the purpose of                                                               | 
Save a soul from death| The eternal significance of restoring a wandering                                                                  | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "Be patient... unto the coming of the Lord" (v.7). Where do you need
   patience right now? Can you wait like a farmer — planting faithfully and
   trusting God's timing for the harvest?

2. "The Lord is very pitiful, and of tender mercy" (v.11). In your
   suffering, do you see God as harsh or compassionate? Job's story ended
   with restoration. Can you trust that God's "end" for you will reveal His
   mercy?

3. "Is any among you afflicted? let him pray" (v.13). What is your first
   response to trouble — worry, complaining, or prayer? James says: pray.
   What about when you're happy? Sing praise.

4. "The effectual fervent prayer of a righteous man availeth much" (v.16).
   Is your prayer life fervent or routine? Passionate or perfunctory?
   Elijah was human like you — but his prayers moved heaven. What's the
   difference?

5. "Confess your faults one to another" (v.16). Do you have someone you can
   confess to — honestly, vulnerably? Confession in community brings
   healing. Who is your safe person?

6. "If any of you do err from the truth, and one convert him" (v.19). Is
   there someone in your life who has wandered from the faith? Will you go
   after them? The stakes are eternal.
